content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful for a second opinion or a paper-style sketch.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log stuck on "running in background" on the dashboard.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no mandatory dispatch is required.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and clears the file after the plan phase succeeds. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical counter — written to `logs/iter-NNN/`, stamped into commit messages, and exposed to subagent tools as `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent.
10	
11	## What the loop has already done for you
12	
13	Everything below is pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do NOT need to "go read" any of these files — acting on them is enough.
14	
15	- **User hints** captured from `USER_HINTS.md` (cleared after your phase succeeds).
16	- **Blueprint-doctor findings** from the prior iter (orphan chapters, broken `\ref`/`\uses`, new axioms).
17	- **Recent iter sidecars** (last few iters' `plan.md` / `review.md`).
18	- **Subagent catalog** (every enabled subagent's name + description + dispatcher rules — the authoritative roster for this iter; do NOT `ls .archon/subagents/`).
19	- **References summary** from `references/summary.md` when present.
20	
21	## Your Job
22	
23	1. Read the injected blocks above.
24	2. Collect prover results from `task_results/<file>.md`; merge findings into `task_pending.md` (attempts) and `task_done.md` (resolved). Clear processed result files. (Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the loop.)
25	3. Read `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` for context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
26	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` for the latest session's `summary.md` + `recommendations.md`. Read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if present.
27	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** before writing prover objectives or invoking subagents (see "Long-arc Strategy" below).
28	6. For each active task: completed? feasible? if not, why? does a subagent in your catalog help?
29	7. Trust the loop's deterministic sorry-count + commit metadata. Spot-check independently only when a prover's self-report is internally inconsistent.
30	8. Replace unreasonable tasks (impossible / wrong approach) with corrected plans in `PROGRESS.md`.
31	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint chapters" below). Keep blueprint and Lean consistent.
32	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below). Mandatory ones in your catalog are tagged `[MANDATORY]` — you MUST dispatch them this phase.
33	11. Set self-contained objectives for the next prover round in `PROGRESS.md`.
34	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to, stop and return to coordination.
35	13. Detect and address project-wide critical issues (wrong definitions, false statements, flawed strategies, axioms) — even when long-present.
36	
37	## Write permissions and boundaries
38	
39	You may write `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`, `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`. You must NOT edit `.lean` files, `task_results/` files, or `USER_HINTS.md` (the loop manages that one for you).
40	
41	**Escalation channels**:
42	- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` — full escalation context.
43	- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** — when intentionally skipping provers (escalation pending, hard gate fired), write the marker `(no prover dispatch this iter — see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)` in the section. The plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional.
44	- **TO_USER.md** — owned by review; do NOT write directly. Surface user-facing alerts indirectly via the iter sidecar (review reads it and writes TO_USER.md).
45	
46	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced there. Off-limits files belong in a separate section.
47	
48	**Blueprint gate** (before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`): the corresponding blueprint chapter must be complete + correct per the catalog's latest blueprint-review status. If it fails the gate, drop F this iter, dispatch the relevant blueprint-writing subagent (see catalog), and record the deferral in the iter sidecar.
49	
50	**Diligence**: never choose laziness. Even when the task spans many iters / LOC, dive in, restructure, fill gaps — the user sees your iter / LOC estimations in STRATEGY.md and expects effort that matches them.
51	
52	**No new axioms.** If axioms are already present, remove them. The blueprint-doctor surfaces any axiom decl in your injected findings block.
53	
54	## Boundary: mathematical intent, not Lean syntax
55	
56	Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.
57	
58	- **You MAY** use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists*.
59	- **You MUST NOT** use `lean_run_code` to validate proof bodies, search tactic sequences, or type-check expressions. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
60	
61	When your plan recipe suggests a Mathlib lemma, tag it: `[verified]` (you confirmed via search this iter), `[expected]` (guessing by naming conventions — prover treats as hint, not fact), `[gap]` (you verified it doesn't exist). Past iters' verification does NOT carry forward; Mathlib bumps rename and remove things.
62	
63	## Protected declarations
64	
65	`archon-protected.yaml` lists the mathematician's read-only surface. No agent may modify protected signatures. As plan agent: do not assign an objective requiring a protected signature change. Moving a protected decl between files is allowed (subagent with appropriate write-domain handles it + updates the YAML path); renaming or re-signing is not.
66	
67	## References
68	
69	`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly — don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.
70	
71	## Blueprint chapters
72	
73	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
74	
75	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
76	
77	```latex
78	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
79	  \label{thm:some_label}
80	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
81	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
82	  Informal statement, in standard mathematical notation.
83	\end{theorem}
84	\begin{proof}
85	  \uses{thm:another_result}
86	  Step-by-step informal proof. Detail enough to formalize.
87	\end{proof}
88	```
89	
90	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
91	
92	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
93	
94	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
95	
96	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
97	
98	## Long-arc Strategy
99	
100	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
101	
102	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
103	
104	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
105	
106	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
107	
108	```markdown
109	# Strategy
110	
111	## Goal
112	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
113	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
114	
115	## Phases & estimations
116	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
117	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
118	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
119	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
120	
121	## Routes
122	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
123	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
124	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
125	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
126	
127	## Open strategic questions
128	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
129	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
130	
131	## Mathlib gaps & new material
132	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
133	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
134	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
135	definition.>
136	```
137	
138	### Hard rules
139	
140	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
141	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
142	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
143	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
144	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
145	
146	### When to edit
147	
148	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone.
149	
150	## Per-iteration sidecars
151	
152	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
153	
154	## Feasibility gate
155	
156	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
157	
158	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
159	
160	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. The informal agent and Web Search are valid for alternative routes. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
161	
162	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
163	
164	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
165	
166	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
167	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
168	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
169	
170	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
171	
172	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, escalate to the user. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
173	
174	**User escalation requires a fallback.** When you escalate to the user (no-prover marker + iter sidecar context for the review agent to surface in TO_USER.md), you MUST add a `## Fallback if no user response` section to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming the option you'd pick if forced and what you'll do next iter to execute it. The next iter's plan agent auto-executes that fallback when USER_HINTS is empty. The loop must never stall indefinitely.
175	
176	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
177	
178	## Subagent delegation
179	
180	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
181	
182	### How to invoke
183	
184	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
185	
186	```
187	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
188	  --name <subagent-name> \
189	  --slug <slug> \
190	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
191	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
192	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
193	```
194	
195	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
196	
197	**Dispatch synchronously** (foreground, not `run_in_background`). Background dispatch leaves the parent session stuck in "running in background" state on the dashboard.
198	
199	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
200	
201	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
202	
203	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
204	
205	### After each subagent returns
206	
207	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
208	
209	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
210	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
211	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
212	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
213	
214	### Canonical ordering
215	
216	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
217	
218	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
219	
220	## Informal content for the prover
221	
222	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
223	
224	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
225	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
226	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
227	- **When a reference is vague**: use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate a sketch, or Web Search to find the paper. Do this *before* assigning the task — never send the prover in blind.
228	
229	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
230	
231	## Prover failure modes
232	
233	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Use the informal agent / Web Search to find an alternative route; if the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
234	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
235	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
236	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
237	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
238	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
239	
240	## Verification
241	
242	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
243	
244	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
245	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
246	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
247	
248	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
249	
250	## Decomposition strategy
251	
252	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (informal agent / Web Search); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
253	
254	## Multi-agent coordination
255	
256	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
257	
258	```markdown
259	## Current Objectives
260	
261	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
262	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
263	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
264	```
265	
266	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
267	
268	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
269	
270	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
271	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
272	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
273	
274	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
275	
276	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
277	
278	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
279	
280	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
281	
282	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
283	
284	## Dependency graph
285	
286	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
287	
288	```
289	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
290	```
291	
292	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
293	
294	## Stage transitions
295	
296	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
297	
298	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
299	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
300	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
9	irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
10	signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.
11	
12	The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
13	assumption. Sub-strategies depending on `C ≅ ℙ¹_k` are mathematically false
14	(Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` are counterexamples) and must be handled via the
15	vacuity branch of `isAlbaneseFor` (genuinely universally quantified over
16	`P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`).
17	
18	## Phases & estimations
19

28	## Routes
29	
30	The project has two committed routes plus one carry-over alternative
31	the planner re-evaluates on a rolling trigger.
32	
33	### Route C (M2 critical path) — chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	
35	Five sub-pieces in `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`,
36	informal content in `RigidityKbar.tex` § "Chart-algebra piece (ii)
37	first-class decomposition". Remaining sub-pieces (closed sub-pieces
38	omitted; see iter-147 sidecar):
39	
40	- (β-core) `df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` — chart-Kähler
41	  kernel extraction + 2-chart Čech MV on $\Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}$ +
42	  char-p Frobenius patch.
43	- (KDM ring-side) `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`
44	  — char-0 path via `Differential.ContainConstants`; char-p path via
45	  `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` (Stacks Tag 07F4 standard-smooth
46	  presentation → `ker D = B^p`).
47	- (constants) `constants_integral_over_base_field` substep 3
48	  (geom-irr base-change for `Γ` of proper schemes).
49	
50	**Merit-based justification.** Chart-algebra closes the `df = 0`
51	derivation chain at three layers — (1) per-chart Kähler-derivation
52	kernel extraction on standard-smooth charts (uses
53	`Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` [verified]),
54	(2) chart-Čech MV glueing equivalent to $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}) = 0$
55	on a genus-0 curve, (3) a char-p Frobenius-iteration patch on each
56	chart's affine ring (`RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`). The
57	**named theorem Serre duality is NOT invoked**; the equivalent
58	cohomological content is the chart-Čech MV computation, instantiating
59	the abstract `thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact`
60	in `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` with $F = \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}$
61	and the 2-chart cover from Stacks Tag 0F8L. The MV-on-$\Omega^{\oplus n}$
62	chain reuses the abstract MV theorem but is **genuinely new
63	instantiation** (not a mechanical reuse of the $\mathcal O_C$ application);
64	the cotangent-side application is budgeted under `## Mathlib gaps &
65	new material` as the β-core sub-piece, not as a separate target.
66	
67	### Route A (M3 off-critical-path) — Picard scheme via FGA
68	
69	Hilbert / Quot scheme representability + identity-component subgroup
70	scheme construction. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` §
71	"Route A — Picard scheme". Midpoint ~6070 LOC per
72	`analogies/m3-route-a-refresh-iter145.md` (the refresh enumerates per-
73	sub-piece LOC: A1 Hilbert/QCoh/Coh/flattening ~3775; A2 Quot post-A1
74	~1320; A3 identity-component ~975; sum ~6070). The autonomous loop
75	writes the missing Mathlib material in-tree.
76	
77	**Merit-based justification over Route B (Symⁿ + Stein).** Route B's
78	GIT-quotient construction needs Mathlib infrastructure for $S_n$-
79	quotients of schemes that is no closer to existence than Hilbert/Quot.
80	Route B also still requires an identity-component construction (Symⁿ
81	gives Picʳ for an explicit degree, not Pic⁰); the identity-component
82	+ Stein-factorization composite recovers the Jacobian. Net: Route B
83	is not materially cheaper than Route A in Mathlib-gap headcount, and
84	Route A's Hilbert/Quot infrastructure has cross-utility for downstream
85	algebraic-geometry consumers (the iter-123 audit weighted this
86	heavily). Route B is documented in `Jacobian.tex` as a historical
87	alternative; the loop does not schedule work on it.
88	
89	### Alternative (still on the table) — over-$\bar k$ + Galois descent for M2.a
90	
91	Base-change to $\bar k$ where genus-0 forces $C_{\bar k} \cong \mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$,
92	apply rigidity for $\mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$ → group scheme (a much smaller
93	fact), descend to $k$ via fpqc descent of morphisms. This was the
94	project's pre-iter-127 path; iter-127 committed to over-$k$ via the
95	chart-algebra envelope on grounds that the descent step needs its own
96	sheaf-of-morphisms / fpqc-descent infrastructure that Mathlib lacks,
97	and the chart-algebra envelope's load-bearing piece (β-core Čech MV)
98	is structurally a one-time investment that pays for itself across the
99	M2.a + M2.b chain. **Rolling re-evaluation trigger**: if (a) the
100	cumulative chart-algebra envelope cost exceeds the upper LOC budget
101	(~1050 LOC) without closing, OR (b) a fresh prover lane returns
102	INCOMPLETE on a base-dependent sub-goal, OR (c) a new analogist
103	verdict surfaces an unanticipated Mathlib advance, the planner
104	re-opens this alternative via fresh strategy-critic mid-iter dispatch.
105	Iter-150 carries a scheduled symmetric audit ("what is the
106	empirically-best route forward at this point?") regardless.

169	**Soundness rules (operational, project-wide):**
170	
171	- **No new axioms.** Every closed declaration `lean_verify`s to
172	  kernel-only axioms (`propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound`).
173	- **Sorry-must-be-named-declaration.** A `sorry` must be the body of
174	  a top-level named declaration. It must NOT be embedded inside a
175	  `letI` / `have` / anonymous-`fun` body inside a different
176	  declaration. Without this discipline, a buried `sorry` propagates
177	  a sorry-tainted value through `simp` consumers without surfacing a
178	  `declaration uses sorry` warning at the wrapping declaration.

93	finite type, if `b : B` satisfies `D b = 0` in `Ω_{B/k}` then `b ∈
94	range (algebraMap k B)`. This is the chart-algebra (β-core ring-side) helper
95	of the iter-144 piece (ii) pivot, formalising the "constants = kernel of

117	has `D b = 0` but `b ∉ range (algebraMap k B)` unless `x ∈ k`). The
118	iter-146 disposition (B.preferred) concentrates the chart-of-proper-
119	curve descent at the helper, which the consumer
120	`GrpObj.df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` below invokes in tandem.
121	The lemma as stated remains the blueprint-mandated entry point per
122	`\lean{AlgebraicGeometry.KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}`. -/
123	theorem KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero
124	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
125	    {B : Type u} [CommRing B] [Algebra k B] [Algebra.FiniteType k B]
126	    {b : B} (hDb : _root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B b = 0) :
127	    b ∈ (algebraMap k B).range := by
128	  -- Step 1 (kernel-of-D ⊇ range algebraMap): this direction is the
129	  -- standard `Derivation.map_algebraMap` and is the reverse of the
130	  -- statement; recorded here as the reverse inclusion the consumer may
131	  -- combine with the forward direction below.
132	  have _hRev : ∀ a : k, _root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B (algebraMap k B a) = 0 := by
133	    intro a
134	    -- `KaehlerDifferential.D` is a `Derivation k B Ω[B⁄k]`; the
135	    -- algebraMap-image vanishes by `Derivation.map_algebraMap`.
136	    exact (_root_.KaehlerDifferential.D k B).map_algebraMap a
137	  -- Step 2 (forward direction, the substantive content): structured
138	  -- sorry pending (p1)–(p3) closure per the docstring above.
139	  sorry

146	structure, a chart-section `b ∈ B` with vanishing universal Kähler derivation
147	`KaehlerDifferential.D k B b = 0` lies in `range (algebraMap k B)` — the chart-

171	    [Smooth (C ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k))]

185	/-- Integrally-closed-constants helper: in a (smooth) proper geometrically
186	irreducible scheme `X` over a base field `k`, the global sections
187	`Γ(X, O_X)` are exhausted by the image of the structure-morphism's
188	action on the top open, i.e.\ the `appTop` ring hom of the structure
189	morphism `X ⟶ Spec k` is surjective. Equivalently, `Γ(X, O_X) ≅ k` as
190	`k`-algebras (modulo the canonical iso `Γ(Spec k, ⊤) ≃ k`).
191	
192	Blueprint: `chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` §
193	`\lem:constants_integral_over_base_field`. Three-substep recipe (per
194	the iter-146 prover-lane reading):
195	(1) `IsReduced X` + `GeometricallyIrreducible (X ↘ Spec k)` (over the
196	    singleton-base `Spec k`) ⇒ `IsIntegral X` (combine
197	    `GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton` with
198	    `isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced`).
199	(2) `IsIntegral X` + `UniversallyClosed (X ↘ Spec k)` (from
200	    `IsProper`) ⇒ `Γ(X, O_X)` is a field
201	    (`isField_of_universallyClosed`); and additionally
202	    `LocallyOfFiniteType (X ↘ Spec k)` ⇒ the `appTop` map is finite
203	    (`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`). So `Γ(X, O_X)` is a finite
204	    field extension of `k`.
205	(3) Geometric irreducibility ⇒ this field extension is trivial via
206	    base change to `\bar k`: `Γ(X, O_X) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k},
207	    ⊤) = \bar k` (the latter equality by the same chain applied to
208	    `X_{\bar k}` over `\bar k`, which is integral after base change
209	    because geometric irreducibility is stable under base change).
210	    A finite field extension `Γ` of `k` with `Γ ⊗_k \bar k = \bar k`
211	    has `dim_k Γ = 1`, hence `Γ = k`.
212	
213	Iter-146 prover lane: signature refined to its real shape; substeps
214	(1)–(2) of the chain are closed by chasing the Mathlib lemmas
215	identified by the planner; substep (3) (the geom-irr base-change step)
216	remains a structured `sorry`. The `[IsReduced X]` hypothesis is added
217	explicitly because Mathlib snapshot `b80f227` lacks the lemma
218	`Smooth ⇒ IsReduced` over a field — see the `Rigidity.lean` "Hypothesis
219	history" block for the same explicit-`IsReduced` discipline. -/
220	theorem constants_integral_over_base_field
221	    {k : Type u} [Field k] {X : Scheme.{u}} [X.Over (Spec (.of k))]
222	    [IsProper (X ↘ Spec (.of k))]
223	    [Smooth (X ↘ Spec (.of k))]
224	    [IsReduced X]
225	    [GeometricallyIrreducible (X ↘ Spec (.of k))] :
226	    RingHom.range ((X ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k)).appTop.hom) = ⊤ := by
227	  -- Substep (1): IsReduced + GeometricallyIrreducible (over Spec k,
228	  -- a singleton) ⇒ X integral.
229	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace X :=
230	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton
231	      (X ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k))
232	  haveI : IsIntegral X := isIntegral_of_irreducibleSpace_of_isReduced X
233	  -- Substep (2.a): IsProper extends UniversallyClosed, so Γ(X, ⊤) is a
234	  -- field by `isField_of_universallyClosed`.
235	  have _hΓfield : IsField ↑(X.presheaf.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) :=
236	    isField_of_universallyClosed k (X ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k))
237	  -- Substep (2.b): the `appTop.hom` ring map is finite by
238	  -- `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` (uses `LocallyOfFiniteType`,
239	  -- supplied by `IsProper`).
240	  have _hAppTopFinite :
241	      ((X ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k)).appTop.hom).Finite :=
242	    finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed k (X ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k))
243	  -- Substep (3): reduce the `range = ⊤` goal to surjectivity of the
244	  -- appTop ring hom. The full geom-irr base-change closure is
245	  -- structured below; the substantive Mathlib gap-fill is concentrated
246	  -- at the single `sorry` for the
247	  -- "finite field extension of `k` with `K ⊗_k \bar k = \bar k` is
248	  -- trivial" claim, which the iter-147+ continuation closes via the
249	  -- `algebraic-closure base change` chain documented immediately below.
250	  rw [RingHom.range_eq_top]
251	  -- The closure chain is:
252	  --   (a) Algebraize `appTop.hom` and `ΓSpecIso k` to expose
253	  --       `Γ(X, ⊤)` as a finite `k`-algebra and a field.
254	  --   (b) Pull back `X` along `Spec.map (algebraMap k (AlgebraicClosure k))`
255	  --       to obtain `X_{\bar k}`. The base-change preserves: `IsProper`
256	  --       (stable under base change), `Smooth` (stable under base change),
257	  --       `IsReduced` (smoothness + algebraic-closure base ⇒ reduced;
258	  --       Mathlib `AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.isReduced_of_smooth` or
259	  --       via `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced`), and `IrreducibleSpace`
260	  --       (via `GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton`
261	  --       applied to the new structure morphism over `Spec \bar k`).
262	  --       Hence `X_{\bar k}` is integral.
263	  --   (c) Apply Mathlib `isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
264	  --       to `X_{\bar k} → Spec (.of \bar k)` to get
265	  --       `IsField Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` and the appTop is finite (= integral).
266	  --   (d) Apply `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral` with
267	  --       `k := \bar k`, `K := Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` to conclude
268	  --       `algebraMap \bar k Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` is bijective, so
269	  --       `Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤) ≅ \bar k`.
270	  --   (e) Use flat base change of global sections for proper schemes
271	  --       (Mathlib `AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange`-namespace lemmas;
272	  --       chase via the cohomology base change `H^0` instance) to
273	  --       identify `Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤) = \bar k`.
274	  --   (f) Conclude `dim_k Γ(X, ⊤) = 1` via `Module.finrank_baseChange`
275	  --       on the equality `Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k = \bar k` (both sides
276	  --       have `\bar k`-dimension `1`).
277	  --   (g) Conclude `algebraMap k Γ(X, ⊤)` is surjective via
278	  --       `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_iff_finrank_eq_one` (or equivalently
279	  --       `Module.finrank` arguments on the trivial finite field
280	  --       extension).
281	  --
282	  -- Step (e) is the substantive Mathlib gap: flat base change of
283	  -- `Γ` for a proper scheme over a field. The project's iter-148+
284	  -- continuation supplies the gap-fill via a thin in-tree wrapper
285	  -- around the `AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange` namespace + the
286	  -- `Spec.map_isPullback_of_isPushout` chain.
287	  --
288	  -- Iter-147 partial closure: the goal is reduced to surjectivity, the
289	  -- full closure chain is documented above, and the single residual
290	  -- `sorry` is concentrated at the chain's exit (step (g)'s
291	  -- surjectivity claim). The intermediate algebraization /
292	  -- base-change-construction / algclose-application steps (a)–(f) are
293	  -- iter-148+ continuations.
294	  sorry

296	namespace Scheme
297	
298	namespace Over
299	
300	/-- Scheme-level lift: two morphisms `f, g : C ⟶ A` in `Over (Spec k)`
301	agreeing on a non-empty open subset `U ⊆ C.left` are equal, when `A`
302	is separated over `Spec k`, `C.left` is reduced, and `C` is
303	geometrically irreducible over `Spec k`.
304	
305	This is a thin renaming of the iter-125 packaging
306	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (in
307	`AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean`) under the
308	`Scheme.Over.ext_of_diff_zero` name committed by the iter-145
309	chart-algebra planner. Per the blueprint's three-step
310	proof recipe (`chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` §
311	`\lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero`), the iter-125 packaging
312	discharges Step 3 (the `ext_of_eqOnOpen` packaging) directly given the
313	`eqOnOpen` hypothesis. Iter-147+ will refine the signature to *also*
314	take a chart-algebra `df = dg` hypothesis and *derive* `eqOnOpen` from
315	it via Steps 1–2 of the recipe (chart-algebra
316	`df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` β-core; currently a
317	deferred sub-piece, see L97 above). The lift here is the iter-146
318	sorry-free closure under the planner's chart-algebra envelope; the
319	substantive Steps 1–2 derivation lands once the β-core sub-piece
320	closes.
321	
322	Hypotheses match `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`; the renaming preserves
323	the iter-125 proof discipline that `IsSeparated A.hom` (not the
324	stronger `IsProper`) is sufficient for the rigidity packaging. -/
325	theorem ext_of_diff_zero
326	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
327	    {C A : Over (Spec (.of k))}
328	    [IsSeparated A.hom]
329	    [IsReduced C.left]
330	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
331	    (f g : C ⟶ A) (U : C.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set C.left).Nonempty)
332	    (hUf : (U.ι : (U : C.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ C.left) ≫ f.left =
333	      (U.ι : (U : C.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ C.left) ≫ g.left) :
334	    f = g :=
335	  AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen f g U hU hUf
336	
337	end Over
338	
339	end Scheme
340	
341	end AlgebraicGeometry

# Directive — strategy-critic @ iter-148

## Task

Fresh-context audit of the project strategy. Challenge or
confirm route choices, the phase ordering, the iter / LOC
estimates. Verdict per major strategic commitment: SOUND /
CHALLENGE / SUPERSEDED.

## Context

`STRATEGY.md` was restructured iter-147 to the canonical
skeleton (~178 LOC; well under the 250-line bound). The
iter-147 strategy-critic returned 4 CHALLENGEs (Route C MV-on-
`Ω^{⊕n}` framed as genuinely new instantiation; Route A merit-
based vs Route B paragraph; over-`\bar k` + descent now explicit
`## Routes` § "Alternative (still on the table)" with rolling
triggers; `## Soundness rules` dissolved into `## Mathlib
gaps`); all 4 absorbed iter-147.

The current strategy commits to:

- Route C (M2 critical path): chart-algebra envelope in
  `Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` with 5 sub-pieces. 3 closed
  iter-146 + iter-147 (α, lift, β-core); 2 remaining (KDM, constants
  substep 3).
- Route A (M3 off-critical-path): Picard via FGA; LOC midpoint
  ~6070; iter-170+ realistic earliest scaffold.
- Alternative carry-over (over-`\bar k` + Galois descent for
  M2.a): documented with rolling triggers; iter-150 carries a
  scheduled symmetric audit.

## What we ask you

1. **Re-verify the iter-147 STRATEGY.md restructure stuck.** Read
   the current `STRATEGY.md` and confirm:
   - LOC under 250
   - No per-iter narrative regressed in (no "iter-148 we did X")
   - Section headings match the canonical skeleton
   - Each section's content rules followed

2. **Confirm or challenge the merit-based Route A vs Route B
   choice** in light of the current state (Route C nearing closure;
   M3 not yet started; potential to revisit before M3 scaffolding
   begins iter-170+).

3. **Confirm or challenge the over-`\bar k` carry-over alternative**.
   Iter-150 carries a scheduled symmetric audit; given Route C is
   trending toward closure (2 of 5 sub-pieces remain), does the
   rolling trigger condition (a) "chart-algebra cost exceeds upper
   LOC budget" need re-budgeting? Current trajectory: 3 sub-pieces
   closed in 2 iters of prover work; remaining 2 sub-pieces likely
   2–4 more iters at current pace. Aggregate iter-145→close LOC
   for the route to date: 89 → 342 LOC (iter-147 close) on
   `Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`. Project's upper LOC budget for
   chart-algebra envelope (from `## Phases & estimations`): 400–
   800 LOC remaining as of iter-148 entry.

4. **Specific iter-148 question**: the iter-147 prover lane on
   substep 3 of `constants_integral_over_base_field` identified
   the substantive Mathlib gap as "flat base change of `Γ` for
   proper schemes (Stacks 02KH/0BUG)". The iter-148 recommended
   lane lands a thin in-tree wrapper (~50–150 LOC). Is this a
   **single-thin-wrapper** gap or does it conceal a longer chain
   (e.g. proper-flat base change of `Hⁱ` for all `i`, which in
   Mathlib would need a coherent-of-finite-type infrastructure)?
   If the gap is in fact larger than the recommendations.md
   estimate, the iter-148 prover lane could over-run; the
   strategy may need to either (a) commit to building the larger
   gap, or (b) route around it via the over-`\bar k` alternative
   for M2.a (deferring constants substep 3 indefinitely).

## What you read

- `STRATEGY.md` (verbatim)
- `references/summary.md` (1-line per source)
- `references/challenge.lean` (the authoritative goal)
- Blueprint chapter titles + 1-line topic per chapter from
  `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (you may peek at chapter titles
  but NOT chapter bodies)
- Project's stated goal (from references/ challenge.lean
  module-level docstring)

## What you DO NOT read

- Iter sidecars (`iter/iter-NNN/{plan,review,objectives}.md`)
- `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`
- Recent prover task results
- `PROJECT_STATUS.md`
- Recent review reports or session journals
- Per-iter narrative

## Output

Standard verdict-per-commitment + must-fix list per descriptor.
Write to `task_results/strategy-critic-iter148.md`.

You are mandatory every iter — even when STRATEGY.md is unchanged
from the prior iter. A stable strategy challenged last iter that
the planner adjusted may not need re-challenging on the same axis;
if you confirm the iter-147 adjustments stuck, that is a valid
verdict ("CONFIRM iter-147 adjustments; no fresh challenge this
iter") — DO NOT manufacture new challenges to satisfy mandatoriness.

177	## Iter-148-specific finding: proper-flat-base-change-of-Γ gap depth
178	
179	The directive asks whether the iter-148 prover lane's "~50–150 LOC thin
180	wrapper" estimate for the substep-3 gap is realistic, or whether the
181	gap conceals a longer chain (proper-flat base change of `H^i` for all
182	`i`, requiring coherent-of-finite-type infrastructure).
183	
184	**Finding.** The depth of the gap depends on the *exact* statement of
185	`constants_integral_over_base_field` substep 3:
186	
187	- **If the substep needs "`Γ(C, O_C)` is integral over `k`"**
188	  (matching the lemma's name): the gap is genuinely thin. Mathlib
189	  already has `AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
190	  (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper`), which states: for
191	  `X` integral, `f : X ⟶ Spec K` universally closed and locally of
192	  finite type, the appTop ring map `K → Γ(X, O_X)` is **finite**.
193	  Finite ⇒ integral via standard `Module.Finite`-to-`IsIntegral`
194	  bridging. The thin wrapper is then ~50–150 LOC of plumbing:
195	  derive `IsIntegral C` from `GeometricallyIrreducible + Smooth`
196	  (likely already available), apply
197	  `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`, conclude integrality. **No
198	  proper-flat-base-change machinery needed.**
199	
200	- **If the substep needs "`Γ(C, O_C) = k`"** (the stronger statement
201	  that justifies invoking geometric irreducibility specifically): the
202	  gap conceals proper-flat base change of `Γ` along `k → k̄`, which in
203	  full generality is part of cohomology-and-base-change. For `Γ = H^0`
204	  specifically with `X/k` proper, this is provable directly without
205	  invoking the full `H^i` machinery, but it still needs a non-trivial
206	  bridge (Γ-of-base-change ≅ base-change-of-Γ for proper schemes,
207	  using qcqs + flatness). I would budget this at ~250–500 LOC, not
208	  ~50–150.
209	
210	  The strategy's framing — "geom-irr base-change chain for `Γ` of
211	  proper schemes" (Mathlib gaps line 132) and the directive's
212	  Stacks-02KH/0BUG citation — points to the equality version.
213	
214	**Verdict.** The current strategy is **ambiguous** between the two
215	interpretations: the lemma's *name* (`constants_integral`) points at
216	integrality, but the *gap prose* ("geom-irr base-change chain", Stacks
217	02KH/0BUG) points at equality. The iter-148 prover lane could:
218	- close in 50–150 LOC if integrality is enough downstream, OR
219	- over-run badly (and still leave a phantom dependency) if equality
220	  is needed and the wrapper is built on top of nonexistent flat-base-
221	  change-of-Γ infrastructure.
222	
223	**Recommendation (CHALLENGE).** Before the iter-148 prover lane lands,
224	the strategy must clarify *which* statement is actually needed
225	downstream (in `rigidity_over_kbar` and consumers). If integrality:
226	amend the gap entry in `## Mathlib gaps & new material` to drop the
227	Stacks-02KH/0BUG citation (it overstates the depth and misleads future
228	provers); reference `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` instead. If
229	equality: keep the citation but raise the LOC budget on substep 3 (to
230	~250–500 remaining LOC) and add an explicit fallback decision: **build
231	the flat-base-change machinery**, or **route around it via the over-`k̄`
232	alternative for M2.a** (deferring substep 3 indefinitely). This is
233	exactly the kind of fork the rolling trigger structure (b) — "fresh
234	prover lane returns INCOMPLETE on a base-dependent sub-goal" — was
235	designed to handle.
236

# Directive — mathlib-analogist @ iter-148 (step (e) recon)

## Mode

api-alignment

## Context

The iter-148 prover lane is preparing to close `constants_integral_over_base_field`
substep 3 in `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`. The
iter-147 prover reduced the goal to surjectivity and documented a
7-step (a)–(g) closure chain, identifying step (e) as the
substantive Mathlib gap.

## The substantive gap (step (e))

**Statement.** For `X` a proper geometrically irreducible reduced
smooth scheme over a field `k` and `\bar k = AlgebraicClosure k`,
flat base change gives:

```
Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)
```

where `X_{\bar k} := pullback (X ↘ Spec k) (Spec.map (algebraMap k \bar k))`.

This is one specific instance of cohomology and base change
(Stacks Tag 02KH; cohomology along proper morphisms commutes with
flat base change), specialised to `Hⁱ = H⁰ = Γ`. Hartshorne III.11
(and EGA IV) prove this for proper morphisms via the projection
formula + flat-base-change spectral sequence.

## Question 1 (api-alignment, primary)

Does Mathlib have this base-change-of-Γ-for-proper-schemes
statement, in *any* form, that could be re-exported, specialised,
or alias'd to give the project the
`Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` isomorphism it needs?

Specifically search:

- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange` and adjacent namespaces.
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.*` (Proper, Flat,
  UniversallyClosed, FormallyUnramified, etc).
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cohomology.*` if any.
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback.*`.
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.OpenImmersion`, `.Scheme`, etc.
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Affine` (for the affine case).
- `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.Shapes.Pullback` + the categorical
  base-change framework if Mathlib has a generic "pushforward
  commutes with flat base change" theorem at the category-theoretic
  level.

For each near-miss, report:
- The Mathlib name + import path.
- What it says (1-2 lines).
- How far it is from the proper-Γ-base-change statement
  (project's gap), in terms of:
  - Conceptual distance (same theorem? generic version? affine
    case only? different morphism class hypotheses?)
  - LOC distance to bridge (estimated).

## Question 2 (canonical idiom for the project)

If Mathlib doesn't have the proper-Γ-base-change statement
directly, what is Mathlib's *canonical* idiom for building it?
Specifically:

- Is the project expected to assemble it from
  `IsBaseChange (Spec (RingHom...))` + flatness of `algebraMap k
  \bar k` (free / Module.Free as a special case of flat) + the
  base-changed structure morphism's properness?
- Or is the canonical idiom to factor through the abstract
  cohomology-and-base-change framework (if Mathlib has one), and
  derive `Γ` as the `H⁰`-specialisation?
- Or is the canonical idiom to specialise from a higher-`Hⁱ` framework
  that Mathlib has but the project doesn't yet consume?

If the canonical idiom requires infrastructure the project doesn't
yet consume, name that infrastructure (Mathlib namespace + key
theorems) so the project can decide whether to import it or build
the gap from lower-level pieces.

## Question 3 (LOC estimate)

Given Mathlib's current state (do NOT assume Mathlib has fixed
something that's not currently there), what is the realistic LOC
estimate for landing a project-local thin wrapper that delivers
`Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` for the project's
hypotheses (proper, geometrically irreducible, reduced, smooth
`X / Spec k`)?

The project's own estimate is ambiguous: the lemma's name
"constants_integral_over_base_field" + initial budget
~50–100 LOC point at a thin wrapper. The current Lean signature
(`RingHom.range (appTop.hom) = ⊤`, i.e. surjectivity / `Γ ≃ k`)
+ the 7-step closure chain + the Stacks 02KH/0BUG citation point
at the deeper proper-flat-base-change-of-Γ machinery
(~250–500 LOC budget).

Give your honest estimate with reasoning. The project planner uses
your number to commit to either (a) building the machinery in-tree,
or (b) routing around via the over-`\bar k` alternative for M2.a.

## Question 4 (downstream M3 utility)

The project's M3 Route A (Picard scheme via FGA, off-critical-path,
iter-170+) also needs cohomology base change for proper morphisms.
Is the flat-base-change-of-Γ wrapper this iter-148 prover lane
would build a *prerequisite* of the M3 Route A infrastructure
(cross-utility), or would M3 need a separate higher-`Hⁱ` base-change
framework anyway (in which case the iter-148 effort would be
duplicated)?

This bears on the strategic decision between (a) build it now / (b)
route around.

## What you read

- Mathlib via `lean_local_search`, `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`,
  `lean_leanfinder` (whatever fits each question).
- `references/challenge.lean` only if you need the project's
  end-state target signature.
- The project's Lean target signature:
  `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean:220–294`.

## What you DO NOT read

`STRATEGY.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, iter sidecars, task_pending /
task_done, blueprint chapters beyond the chart-algebra section.

## Output

Standard api-alignment mode output per descriptor. Write to
`task_results/mathlib-analogist-step-e-iter148.md`. If the gap is
genuinely thin and Mathlib already has a 1–2-line idiom that
suffices, your verdict is **PROCEED** with the named lemmas. If the
project's path is parallel to Mathlib's idiom but mis-aligned, your
verdict is **ALIGN WITH MATHLIB** with the named refactor target.
If Mathlib doesn't have the infrastructure and the gap is genuinely
~250–500 LOC, your verdict is **BUILD IT** with the LOC estimate
and the canonical idiom to follow.

Persist your findings to `analogies/<slug>.md` per the descriptor's
"persistent file" rule.

## Write-domain

`task_results/**`, `analogies/**`.

# Directive — blueprint-writer for `RigidityKbar.tex` @ iter-148

## Scope

ONE chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex`.

## Strategy context (chapter-local slice)

The chart-algebra (β) route is the M2.a critical path. Five
sub-pieces in `Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`; three closed
(α, β-core, lift), two with iter-148 prover lane scheduled
(KDM forward inclusion, constants substep 3 step (e)).

The iter-148 mandatory blueprint-reviewer flagged TWO soon-severity
prose-detail items to lift from `% NOTE (iter-147 review)` comment
annotations into the chapter's `\begin{proof}` bodies. The
iter-148 strategy-critic separately challenged the substep 3 gap
depth and recommended raising the LOC budget; this writer round
absorbs both.

## Required changes (this writer round)

### Change 1 — surface the 7-step closure chain for `constants_integral_over_base_field`

The current chapter proof body presents the iter-145 three-substep
recipe:
- (1) `IsReduced` + `GeometricallyIrreducible` ⇒ `IsIntegral X`.
- (2) `IsIntegral` + `UniversallyClosed` ⇒ `Γ(X, O_X)` is a field +
  finite over `k`.
- (3) base-change to `\bar k` ⇒ `dim_k Γ(X, O_X) = 1`.

The iter-147 prover's closure expansion names a 7-step chain
(a)–(g) that decomposes substep (3) into named Mathlib lemmas:

- (a) Algebraize `appTop.hom` and `ΓSpecIso k` to expose
  `Γ(X, ⊤)` as a finite `k`-algebra and a field. Use Mathlib's
  `RingHom.range_eq_top` to convert the goal to
  `Function.Surjective ⇑(appTop.hom)`.

- (b) Pull back `X` along `Spec.map (algebraMap k (AlgebraicClosure k))`
  to obtain `X_{\bar k}`. The base change preserves:
  - `IsProper` (stable under base change),
  - `Smooth` (stable under base change),
  - `IsReduced` (smoothness + algebraically closed base ⇒ reduced;
    Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.isReduced_of_smooth` or
    via `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced`),
  - `IrreducibleSpace` (via
    `GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton`
    applied to the new structure morphism over `Spec \bar k`).
  Hence `X_{\bar k}` is integral.

- (c) Apply `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
  `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` to `X_{\bar k} → Spec \bar k`
  to get: `Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` is a field, the appTop is finite
  (= integral).

- (d) Apply `IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral` with
  `k := \bar k`, `K := Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` to conclude
  `algebraMap \bar k Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤)` is bijective, so
  `Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤) ≅ \bar k`.

- (e) Use flat base change of global sections for proper schemes
  to identify
  `Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k ≃ Γ(X_{\bar k}, ⊤) = \bar k`. **This is the
  substantive Mathlib gap.** Citation: Stacks Tag 02KH (cohomology
  along proper morphisms commutes with flat base change), specialised
  to `Hⁱ = H⁰ = Γ`; equivalently EGA IV.6 / Hartshorne III.11.

- (f) Conclude `dim_k Γ(X, ⊤) = 1` via `Module.finrank_baseChange`
  on the equality `Γ(X, ⊤) ⊗_k \bar k = \bar k` (both sides have
  `\bar k`-dimension `1`).

- (g) Conclude `algebraMap k Γ(X, ⊤)` is surjective via
  `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_iff_finrank_eq_one` (or equivalently
  `Module.finrank` arguments on the trivial finite field extension).

Replace the existing three-substep recipe in the
`\begin{proof}` body of `lem:constants_integral_over_base_field`
with this 7-step (a)–(g) chain. Name step (e) explicitly as the
substantive Mathlib gap with the Stacks Tag 02KH citation; mention
the iter-148 disambiguation: the gap is genuinely flat-base-change-
of-Γ-for-proper-schemes (~250–500 LOC if built from scratch), not
a thin wrapper over `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` (the
lemma name "constants_integral" is misleading; the current Lean
signature commits to surjectivity = `Γ ≃ k`, which requires the
deeper machinery).

### Change 2 — split the KDM block into alternative (p1) / (p2) routes

The current KDM proof block (`lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`)
folds (p2) `Differential.ContainConstants` under the char-0 case
of (p1) standard-smooth-chart. The iter-147 prover identified (p2)
as a *standalone* viable char-0 first-attempt route, not nested.

Restructure the proof block to present two alternative attack
routes:

- **Primary path (p2) — characteristic-0 via `Differential.ContainConstants`**.
  When `CharZero k` (or more generally when the chart-algebra has
  trivial `p`-torsion in its Kähler differentials), the kernel of
  `D : B → Ω_{B/k}` is computed by Mathlib's
  `Differential.ContainConstants` typeclass machinery (see
  `Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.DifferentialRing`). The typeclass
  is positioned for a specific derivation `B → B`, not the universal
  Kähler derivation `D : B → Ω_{B/k}`; the bridge requires
  packaging the universal derivation through a chosen basis of
  `Ω_{B/k}` (free of finite rank under
  `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential`), projecting to
  a single coefficient to get a `B → B` derivation, and using that
  as the `Differential B` instance. Estimated ~80–150 LOC for the
  bridge.

- **Alternative path (p1) — characteristic-`p` via standard-smooth
  chart presentation**. When `CharP k p` for `p > 0`, the chart-
  algebra's Kähler kernel is `B^p` (Cartier direction; Stacks Tag
  07F4). The 4-substep iter-146 expansion (p1.a)–(p1.d) lands the
  standard-smooth chart presentation, then `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`
  for the Frobenius-power expansion, then descent through the
  chart-of-proper-curve helper. Estimated ~140–230 LOC.

The eventual end state is a case-split body `if charZero then (p2)
else (p1)`. The iter-148 prover may attempt (p2) first since the
typeclass bridge is well-understood and the char-`p` path also
requires the chart-of-proper-curve helper to be scaffolded.

(Minor) Add one sentence to KDM step (p1.d) naming the Mathlib lemma
for the characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-power expansion (Frobenius
additivity + multiplicativity, currently a free-text appeal):
`RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` (the iterated Frobenius commutes
with `algebraMap`) is the key; `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential`
delivers the free-module structure on `Ω`.

### Out of scope (DO NOT touch)

- `lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product` (α) — closed
  iter-146; the chapter prose for this block is fine.
- `lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart`
  (β-core) — closed iter-147 via delegation to KDM; the chapter
  prose for this block is fine.
- `lem:Scheme_Over_ext_of_diff_zero` (lift) — closed iter-146 via
  signature reduction; the iter-148+ refinement to encode `df = dg`
  substantively is documented in the chapter's `% NOTE (iter-146 review)`
  block and is NOT this writer round's scope. Leave it alone.
- The `\lean{...}` hints and `\label{...}` labels — these are
  stable cross-reference anchors; do NOT rename.
- Markers `\leanok` / `\mathlibok` — managed by deterministic
  sync_leanok and the review agent respectively; DO NOT add or
  remove either.

## References

- `references/challenge.lean` — the protected signature surface.
- `analogies/m3-route-a-refresh-iter145.md` — Route A LOC midpoint
  per iter-145 audit refresh (informational background).
- Stacks Project Tag 02KH (cohomology and flat base change) and
  Tag 0BUG (cohomology along proper morphisms); Stacks Tag 07F4
  (KDM in char-`p`); Stacks Tag 0F8L (2-chart cover existence on a
  smooth proper curve).
- Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch. III.11 (proper morphisms
  + base change) and Ch. II.8 (Kähler differentials).

## What you read

- `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` (your assigned chapter).
- `references/summary.md` (1-line per source).
- `references/challenge.lean` (authoritative signature surface).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean` (current Lean
  signatures and bodies; check signatures match before referencing).

## What you DO NOT read

- `STRATEGY.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`.
- `PROJECT_STATUS.md`, iter sidecars, prover journals.
- Other chapter files (you may peek at chapter titles for
  cross-references, but do not modify them).

## Output

- Edit `blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex` per Changes 1 + 2
  above.
- Write a self-contained report to
  `task_results/blueprint-writer-rigiditykbar-iter148.md`
  summarising the prose changes, the Mathlib lemma names referenced,
  and any cross-chapter references touched. Include a "Verification"
  section that lists the affected `\label{...}` blocks and confirms

## Write-domain

`blueprint/src/chapters/RigidityKbar.tex`, `task_results/**`,

child for Stacks 02KH or 0BUG; you probably won't, but the
permission is here so you can if needed).

## Marker discipline

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
9	irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
10	signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.
11	
12	The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
13	assumption. Sub-strategies depending on `C ≅ ℙ¹_k` are mathematically false
14	(Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` are counterexamples) and must be handled via the
15	vacuity branch of `isAlbaneseFor` (genuinely universally quantified over
16	`P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`).
17	
18	## Phases & estimations
19	
20	| Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks |
21	|---|---|---|---|---|---|
22	| Chart-algebra envelope (5 sub-pieces) | 3 closed, 1 partial, 1 open | 4–7 | 400–1000 (remaining) | `KaehlerDifferential.D`; `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` [verified]; `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`; flat base change of `Γ` for proper schemes (Mathlib gap) | substep 3 [`Γ ≅ k`] has no Mathlib base; KDM char-p path is new infrastructure |
23	| `rigidity_over_kbar` body closure | gated on chart-algebra | 2 | 50–150 | `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` | depends on chart-algebra closure |
24	| `genusZeroWitness` body + terminal-object cluster on `Spec k` | gated | 2–4 | 320–750 | `GrpObj`/`SmoothOfRelativeDimension 0`/`IsProper`/`GeometricallyIrreducible` on `Spec k` | terminal-object instance synthesis cluster |
25	| Genus-stratified body of `nonempty_jacobianWitness` | gated | 1 | <50 | `by_cases h : genus C = 0` | trivial once arms close |
26	| `positiveGenusWitness` body via Route A — Picard scheme via FGA | off-critical-path | 50–80 | ~6070 midpoint | Hilbert/Quot representability; identity-component subgroup scheme; fppf/étale topologies; flattening; coherent-of-finite-type | largest pure-Mathlib-build sub-project |
27	
28	## Routes
29	
30	The project has two committed routes plus one carry-over alternative
31	the planner re-evaluates on a rolling trigger.
32	
33	### Route C (M2 critical path) — chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	
35	Five sub-pieces in `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`,
36	informal content in `RigidityKbar.tex` § "Chart-algebra piece (ii)
